title: Dog Swallowed Pennies: Zinc Toxicity Guide & Emergency Steps
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a penny, they are at risk for zinc toxicity. This is a high-priority emergency. Learn the symptoms and triage steps now.
content_html:
What is Zinc Toxicity?

Zinc toxicity occurs when a dog ingests items containing high levels of zinc, most commonly pennies minted after 1982. When a penny sits in the acidic environment of the stomach, it dissolves rapidly, releasing zinc into the bloodstream. This causes a life-threatening condition where red blood cells are destroyed, leading to severe anemia, liver damage, and kidney failure.

Is it an Emergency?

Yes. This is a High Urgency emergency. Unlike some foreign objects that may pass through the digestive tract, pennies are chemically dangerous. If your dog has swallowed a penny, you must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. Delayed treatment increases the risk of permanent organ damage or death.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo of any remaining coins or even the dog's vomit can be incredibly helpful for triage. A photo of the coin's date can confirm if it is a high-zinc penny (post-1982). Additionally, photos of the dog's gums or urine can help the vet assess the severity of red blood cell destruction before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of zinc poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Initial symptoms often include vomiting, loss of appetite, and lethargy. As the condition progresses, you may notice pale or yellow gums (jaundice) and orange-colored urine.

faqs.question: How many pennies does it take to hurt a dog?
faqs.answer: For small dogs, even a single penny can cause fatal zinc toxicity. While larger dogs might require more for the same effect, any ingestion is considered an emergency regardless of the dog's size.

faqs.question: Will my vet need to perform surgery?
faqs.answer: The vet will first perform X-rays to locate the coin. Removal is typically necessary and is usually done via endoscopy or surgery to prevent further toxin absorption.
